summary: Gruber draws an extended parallel between manipulating VisionOS's interface through pinch gestures and using the Force in Star Wars. He argues that VisionOS's indirect manipulation — looking at UI elements and pinching to move, resize, or activate them — feels so precise and natural that it evokes a near-physical, telekinetic sensation. He traces the iconic pinch gesture through Star Wars scenes from Obi-Wan's mind trick to Vader's Force choke, noting that George Lucas defined how generations imagined telekinesis would feel. The piece also connects this to Apple's longstanding commitment to making touch tracking feel like direct physical manipulation, a craft advantage he says Android still lacks.
key_points: VisionOS's pinch-gesture interaction feels like telekinesis — indirect manipulation so precise it creates the illusion of a mind-to-object connection
The iPhone's touch-tracking accuracy created the illusion of direct manipulation from day one, and Android still hasn't matched the physics of that illusion
George Lucas's hand-gesture portrayal of the Force in Star Wars maps perfectly onto VisionOS's pinch gestures, right down to the finger-thumb pinch
When illusions are realistic enough, the brain accepts them as real — some people physically feel the butterfly land on their finger in the Encounter Dinosaurs demo
Apple's 'Force Touch' trademark was wasted on a pressure-sensitive trackpad when it perfectly describes VisionOS's gesture-based interaction
money_quotes: When an illusion is realistic enough, your brain accepts it as real, even though it's not — and even though you know it's not.
What it feels like is using the Force.
I swear to you, I can almost feel the telekinetic connection with UI elements in VisionOS.
It's a hint, a whiff, of tension — between not just my hands and the virtual elements I'm manipulating, but between my mind and those elements.
George Lucas didn't conceive of telekinesis, but his portrayal of it made it seem real, and defined how my and subsequent generations imagined they would invoke such an ability if they could.
It didn't feel like you were issuing an indirect shortcut gesture; it felt like you were directly manipulating whatever it was you were sliding or scrolling on screen.
It's not because they don't have high-enough frame rates or low-enough latency for touch input. It's just that the math is off. The physics aren't quite right. It's about craftsmanship.
This is what Apple should have saved the term 'Force Touch' for.
key_insight: VisionOS's pinch-gesture interaction is so precise and lag-free that it transcends indirect manipulation and creates the visceral illusion of telekinesis — the same Force-like power George Lucas made generations dream about.
